content:
# Romans Chapter 6 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Romans Chapter 6 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 6:1 — "continue in sin" vs. "remain in the sin" vs. "keep on sinning"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Shall we remain in the sin that the grace may increase?" | Literal — epimenomen te hamartia; definite article "the sin" preserved |
| LOGOS | "Are we to continue in sin that grace may abound?" | Formal equivalent — drops the article for English idiom |
| PNEUMA | "should we keep on sinning so that God can show us more and more of his wonderful grace?" | Dynamic — "keep on sinning"; adds "God can show us more and more of his wonderful" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's retention of "the sin" (with article) is textually faithful but awkward in English. PNEUMA's expansion significantly alters the question — Paul asks about sin increasing grace, not about God "showing" grace.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:2 — "By no means!" vs. "May it never be!" vs. "Of course not!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "May it never be!" | Traditional rendering of me genoito |
| LOGOS | "By no means!" | Formal idiomatic equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Of course not!" | Dynamic colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "By no means!" — consistent with the decision in chapter 3. Me genoito demands a strong negative. "Of course not!" (PNEUMA) is too casual for Paul's emphatic rejection.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Applied consistently throughout the chapter.

---

## 6:4 — "walk in newness of life" vs. "live new lives"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "so also we might walk in newness of life" | Literal — peripateo ("walk") + kainoteti zoes ("newness of life") |
| LOGOS | "we too might walk in newness of life" | Formal — identical to EMET |
| PNEUMA | "now we also may live new lives" | Dynamic — "live new lives" replaces the walk metaphor and "newness of life" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's rendering. "Walk" (peripateo) is Paul's characteristic metaphor for daily conduct — it appears repeatedly in his letters. "Newness of life" is a distinctive Pauline phrase that should be preserved rather than paraphrased as "new lives."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 6:6 — "brought to nothing" vs. "rendered powerless" vs. "lose its power"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that the body of the sin might be rendered powerless" | Literal — katargeo = "to render inoperative, make powerless" |
| LOGOS | "that the body of sin might be brought to nothing" | Formal — "brought to nothing" for katargeo |
| PNEUMA | "so that sin might lose its power in our lives" | Dynamic — personalizes "sin" and removes "body of sin" language |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Katargeo means "to make ineffective, to nullify." "Brought to nothing" captures the decisive nature of the act. EMET's "rendered powerless" is also strong. PNEUMA loses "the body of sin" (to soma tes hamartias), which is a key Pauline concept — the whole self as organized by sin.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:7 — "set free from sin" vs. "justified from the sin"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] having died has been justified from the sin" | Literal — dedikaiootai apo tes hamartias; "justified" preserves the forensic verb |
| LOGOS | "one who has died has been set free from sin" | Formal — "set free" interprets dikaioo in the context of liberation |
| PNEUMA | "when we died with Christ we were set free from the power of sin" | Dynamic — adds "with Christ" and "the power of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "set free." This is a notable case where dikaioo (normally "justify") carries a liberation sense — "acquitted/discharged from sin's jurisdiction." EMET's "justified" is lexically accurate but could confuse readers in a chapter about freedom from sin's power. PNEUMA's additions are interpretive.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's observation about dikaioo's forensic sense is noted.

---

## 6:11 — "consider" vs. "reckon"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "reckon yourselves to be dead indeed to the sin" | Literal — logizesthe, "reckon/count" — the same word from chapter 4 |
| LOGOS | "consider yourselves dead to sin and alive to God" | Formal — "consider" for logizesthe |
| PNEUMA | "you also should consider yourselves to be dead to the power of sin and alive to God" | Dynamic — adds "the power of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "consider." While "reckon" (EMET) connects to the logizomai theme from chapter 4 (the accounting metaphor), "consider" is more natural in the imperative context of this verse. PNEUMA's addition of "the power of" is interpretive.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:13 — "instruments" vs. "weapons" — hopla

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "neither present the members of you as weapons of unrighteousness to the sin, but present yourselves to the God... and the members of you as weapons of righteousness" | Literal — hopla = "weapons/arms" (military) |
| LOGOS | "Do not present your members to sin as instruments for unrighteousness... your members to God as instruments for righteousness" | Formal — "instruments" for hopla |
| PNEUMA | "Do not let any part of your body become an instrument of evil to serve sin. Instead, give yourselves completely to God... use your whole body as an instrument to do what is right" | Dynamic — "instrument" with expansion; "part of your body" for "members" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "instruments." Hopla can mean either "weapons" (military) or "instruments" (tools). Both EMET and LOGOS are defensible. "Instruments" is broader and fits the context where Paul addresses daily moral life, not just warfare. The military dimension returns in 13:12 (hopla photos, "armor of light").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's military reading is noted.

---

## 6:14 — "dominion" vs. "lordship" vs. "master"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "sin will not have lordship over you" | Literal — kurieusei, from kurios ("lord") |
| LOGOS | "sin will have no dominion over you" | Formal — "dominion" for kurieusei |
| PNEUMA | "Sin is no longer your master" | Dynamic — personalizes sin as "master" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "dominion." Kurieuo means "to exercise lordship over." "Dominion" captures the authoritative/ruling sense. EMET's "lordship" is accurate but in a passage about Christ as Lord (kurios), using "lordship" for sin could create confusion. PNEUMA's "master" is effective but shifts from the abstract to the concrete.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:17 — "standard of teaching to which you were committed" vs. "form of teaching to which you were handed over" vs. "this teaching we have given you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you obeyed from [the] heart the form of teaching to which you were handed over" | Literal — typon didaches eis hon paredothete; "handed over" (paradidomi) — same verb used for Christ's death |
| LOGOS | "you who were once slaves of sin have become obedient from the heart to the standard of teaching to which you were committed" | Formal — "standard" for typos; "committed" for paradidomi |
| PNEUMA | "Once you were slaves of sin, but now you wholeheartedly obey this teaching we have given you" | Dynamic — simplifies entirely; "we have given you" reverses the passive |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The phrase is theologically dense: believers were "handed over" (paradidomi) to a "pattern" (typos) of teaching — just as Christ was "handed over" for sins. EMET preserves this verbal connection. LOGOS's "committed" is weaker but readable. PNEUMA's "we have given you" entirely reverses the passive voice, losing the sense that believers were delivered into a tradition, not that the tradition was delivered to them.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's paradidomi observation is noted.

---

## 6:19 — "natural limitations" vs. "weakness of the flesh" vs. "weakness of your human nature"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "because of the weakness of the flesh of you" | Literal — astheneian tes sarkos |
| LOGOS | "because of your natural limitations" | Formal — interprets "weakness of the flesh" as general human limitation |
| PNEUMA | "Because of the weakness of your human nature" | Dynamic — "human nature" for sarx |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Paul says he uses the slavery analogy because of their "fleshly weakness" — their limited capacity to grasp abstract theology. "Natural limitations" communicates this pedagogical adjustment. PNEUMA's "human nature" imports a broader anthropological meaning for sarx that goes beyond Paul's intent here.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:22 — "sanctification" vs. "holiness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you have the fruit of you for holiness, and the end — eternal life" | Literal — hagiasmon, "sanctification/holiness" |
| LOGOS | "the fruit you get leads to sanctification and its end, eternal life" | Formal — "sanctification" for hagiasmos |
| PNEUMA | "Now you do those things that lead to holiness and result in eternal life" | Dynamic — "holiness" for hagiasmos |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "sanctification." Hagiasmos specifically means the process of being made holy (sanctification), not the state of being holy (holiness). The process language matters in a chapter about the ongoing transformation of believers.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:23 — "wages" vs. "wages" — consistent rendering of opsonia

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the wages of the sin [is] death, but the gift of the God [is] eternal life in Christos Yeshua the Lord of us" | Literal — opsonia (soldier's pay) vs. charisma (grace-gift) |
| LOGOS | "the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life through Christ Jesus our Lord" | Dynamic — nearly identical to LOGOS; "through" instead of "in" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. All three translators substantially agree on this iconic verse. The only divergence is PNEUMA's "through" (dia) vs. "in" (en) Christ Jesus. The Greek has en, so "in" is correct.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor preposition divergence in PNEUMA noted.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| EMET too literal / wooden for English | 4 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA too expansive / interpretive | 8 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS or EMET |
| LOGOS and SOPHIA nearly identical | 10 | LOGOS rendering adopted as consensus baseline |
| Key theological term at stake | 3 | katargeo (v.6); dikaioo (v.7); hopla (v.13) |
| Military metaphor preservation | 1 | EMET's "weapons" noted but "instruments" adopted |
| High agreement across all three | 4 | vv.8-10, v.23 — minimal divergence |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Romans 6 shows notably higher agreement among the three translators compared to earlier chapters, particularly in the baptismal theology section (vv.3-5) and the concluding wage/gift contrast (v.23). This likely reflects the chapter's concrete imagery (baptism, slavery, wages) which constrains the range of acceptable renderings.

The most significant divergences cluster around two issues: (1) the degree to which PNEUMA adds interpretive content ("the power of sin" for "sin," "instrument of evil" for "weapon of unrighteousness"), and (2) key Greek terms where lexical precision matters theologically (katargeo in v.6, dikaioo in v.7, hopla in v.13, hagiasmos in v.22).

EMET's most valuable contribution in this chapter is the observation about paradidomi in v.17 — the same verb used for Christ being "handed over" is used for believers being "handed over" to the pattern of teaching. This verbal echo links Christology and discipleship. LOGOS continues to provide the most balanced renderings for the consensus.

---

*Log compiled by SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
